For the parametric regression problem, analogs of the residual plots
described in Chapter 11 can be made with a redefinition of the various
residuals to incorporate the parametric form of the baseline hazard rates.
The first such residual is the Cox-Snell residual that provides a check
of the overall fit of the model. The Cox-Snell residual, $r _ { j } ,$ is defined by
$r _ { j } = \widehat { H } \left( T _ { j } | \mathrm { Z } _ { j } \right) ,$ where $\widehat { H }$ is the fitted model. If the model fits the data
then the $r _ { j }$ should have a standard $\left( \lambda = 1 \right)$ exponential distribution,
so that a hazard plot of $r _ { j }$ versus the Nelson-Aalen estimator of the
cumulative hazard of the $r _ { i }$ should be $a$ straight line with slope 1. For
the four models considered in this chapter, the Cox-Snell residuals are


<table>
<tr>
<td>Exponential</td>
<td colspan="2">$r _ { i } = \widehat { \lambda } t _ { i } \exp \left\{ \widehat { \beta } ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i } \right\} ,$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Weibull</td>
<td colspan="2">$\widehat { \lambda } \exp \left( \widehat { \beta } ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i } \right) t _ { 1 } ^ { \widehat { \alpha } } ,$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Log logistic</td>
<td></td>
<td>$$\ln \left[ \frac { 1 } { 1 + \widehat { \lambda } \exp \left( \widehat { \beta } ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i } \right) t _ { j } ^ { \widehat { \alpha } } } \right] ,$$</td>
</tr>
</table>


<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="415" -->
<!-- PageHeader="12.5 Diagnostic Methods for Parametric Models" -->

and

Log normal
$\ln \left[ 1 - \Phi \left( \frac { \ln T _ { j } - \widehat { \mu } - \widehat { \gamma } ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { j } } { \widehat { \sigma } } \right) \right] .$
ˆ

.

Examination of model fit with the Cox-Snell residuals is equivalent
to that done using the so-called standardized residuals based on the $\log$
linear model representation. Here, we define the standardized residuals
by analogy to those used in normal theory regression as

$$s _ { j } = \frac { \ln T _ { j } - \widehat { \mu } - \widehat { \gamma } ^ { t } Z _ { j } } { \widehat { \sigma } } .$$

If the Weibull model holds, then, these residuals should be a censored
sample from the standard extreme value distribution $\left( 1 2 . 2 . 2 \right) ;$ if the $\log$
logistic distribution holds, these are a censored sample from a standard
logistic distribution (12.3.1); and if the log normal distribution holds,
these are a censored sample from a standard normal distribution. The
hazard plot techniques discussed earlier can be used to check if the
standardized residuals have the desired distribution. However, the haz-
ard plots obtained are exactly those obtained by the exponential hazard
plot for the Cox-Snell residuals.

EXAMPLE 12.2

(continued): In Figures 12.6-12.9, the cumulative hazard plots for the
Cox-Snell residuals are shown for the exponential, Weibull, log logistic
and log normal regression models for the laryngeal cancer data. We see
from these plots that all four models give reasonable fits to the data,
the best being the log normal and log logistic models.

In Chapter 11, the martingale and deviance residuals were defined for
Cox regression models. For a parametric model, the martingale residual
is defined by $M _ { j } = 8 _ { j } - r _ { j }$ and the deviance residual by

$$D _ { j } = \mathrm { s i g n } \left[ M _ { j } \right] \left\{ - 2 \left[ M _ { j } + \delta _ { j } \ln \left( \delta _ { j } - M _ { j } \right) \right] \right\} ^ { 1 / 2 } .$$

As for the Cox model, the martingale residual is an estimate of the ex-
cess number of deaths seen in the data, but not predicted by the model.
In the parametric case, note that the derivation of $M _ { 1 }$ as a martingale
does not hold but, because the residuals are similar in form to those for
the Cox model, the name carries through. The deviance residuals are
an attempt to make the martingale residuals more symmetric about 0. If
the model is correct, then, the deviance residuals should look like ran-
dom noise. Plots of either the martingale or deviance residuals against
time, observation number, or acceleration factor provides a check of
the model's adequacy. The discussion of how to use these residuals
in Chapter 11 carries over to the parametric case. We shall illustrate

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="416" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 12 Inference for Parametric Regression Models" -->

the use of the deviance residuals in the following continuation of Ex-
ample 12.2.

EXAMPLE 12.2
(continued): We shall examine the fit of the log logistic regression
model to the laryngeal cancer data using the deviance residuals. Fig-
ure 12.10 is a plot of the deviance residuals versus time on study. Here,
we see that the deviance residuals are quite large for small times and
that they decrease with time. This suggests that the model underesti-
mates the chance of dying for small t and overestimates this chance for
large t. However, there are only a few outliers early, which may cause
concern about the model. The deviance residual plots for the other
three models are quite similar.

### Practical Note

1\. Martingale and deviance residuals for these parametric models are
available in S-Plus.


#### Theoretical Notes

1\. Further work on graphical checks for the parametric regression mod-
els can be found in Weissfeld and Schneider (1990) and Escobar and
Meeker (1992).

2\. It is possible to define a score residual for the various parametric
models similar to that presented in section 12.6. To illustrate how

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="418" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 12 Inference for Parametric Regression Models" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 12.8 Cox-Snell residuals to assess the $f i t$ of the log logistic regression model for the laryngeal cancer data set</figcaption>

3.0

2.5

Estimated Cumulative Hazard Rates

2.0

1.5

1.0

0.5

0.0

0.0

0.5

1.0

1.5

2.0

Residual

</figure>


this is done, consider the Weibull regression problem with a single
covariate Z. The contribution of an individual with covariate $Z _ { j }$ to
the likelihood is given by

$$L _ { j } = \left[ \exp \left( \beta Z _ { j } \right) \lambda \alpha t _ { j } ^ { \alpha - 1 } \right] ^ { \delta _ { j } } \exp \left[ - \lambda \exp \left( \beta Z _ { j } \right) T _ { j } ^ { \alpha } \right] .$$

The score residual for $\lambda$ is given by

$$\frac { \partial \ln L _ { j } } { \partial \lambda } = \frac { \delta _ { j } } { \lambda } - \exp \left( \beta Z _ { j } \right) T _ { j } ^ { \alpha } ,$$

for $\alpha ,$

$$\frac { \partial \ln L _ { j } } { \partial \alpha } = + \frac { \delta _ { j } } { \alpha } + \delta _ { j } \ln T _ { j } - \lambda \exp \left( \beta Z _ { j } \right) T _ { j } ^ { \alpha } \ln T _ { j } ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="419" -->
<!-- PageHeader="12.6 Exercises" -->


<figure>
<figcaption>Figure 12.9 Cox-Snell residuals to assess the $f i t$ of the log normal regression model for the laryngeal cancer data set</figcaption>

3.0

2.5

Estimated Cumulative Hazard Rates

2.0

1.5

1.0

0.5

0.0

0.0

0.5

1.0

1.5

2.0

Residual

</figure>


and for $\beta ,$

$$\frac { \partial \ln L _ { j } } { \partial \beta } = \delta _ { j } Z _ { j } - \lambda Z _ { j } \exp \left( \beta Z _ { j } \right) T _ { j } ^ { \alpha } .$$

These residuals can be used, as in section 11.6, to examine the
influence of a given observation on the estimates. See Collett (1994)
for additional detail. These residuals are available in S-Plus.

(a) For both the aneuploid and diploid groups fit a Weibull model to
the data. Find the maximum likelihood estimates of $\lambda$ and a, and
their standard errors.

(b) For both groups, test the hypothesis that the shape parameter, «, is
equal to 1 by both the Wald and likelihood ratio tests.

(c) Find the maximum likelihood estimates of the median survival for
both groups. Use the delta method to find an estimate of the stan-
dard error of your estimates.

(d) Fit a Weibull regression model to this data with a single covariate,
$Z ,$ that is equal to 1 if the patient had an aneuploid DNA profile
and 0 otherwise. Test the hypothesis of no effect of ploidy on
survival using the likelihood ratio test and the Wald test. Find a
point estimate and 95% confidence interval for the relative risk of
death for an aneuploid tumor as compared to a diploid tumor. Also
find a point estimate and a 95% confidence for the acceleration
factor. Provide an interpretation of this factor.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="421" -->
<!-- PageHeader="12.6 Exercises" -->

12.2
In section 1.4 the times to first exit-site infection (in months) of patients
with renal insufficiency were reported. In the study 43 patients had a
surgically placed catheter (Group 1) and 76 patients had a percutaneous
placement of their catheter (Group 0).

(a) For both groups fit a Weibull model to the data. Find the maximum
likelihood estimates of $\lambda$ and $\alpha ,$ and their standard errors.

(b) For both groups test the hypothesis that the shape parameter, a, is
equal to 1 using the likelihood ratio test and the Wald test.

(c) Find the maximum likelihood estimates and 95% confidence inter-
vals for the two survival functions at 5 months after placement of
the catheter. Compare these estimates to those obtained using the
product-limit estimator.

(d) Fit a Weibull regression model to this data with a single covariate, $Z ,$
that indicates group membership. Test the hypothesis of no effect
of catheter placement on the time to exit site infection. Find point
estimates and 95% confidence intervals for the relative risk and the
acceleration factor for exit site infections. Provide an interpretation
of these quantities.

12.3
In section 1.10, times to death or relapse (in days) are given for 23 non-
Hodgkin's lymphoma (NHL) patients, 11 receiving an allogeneic (Allo)
transplant from an HLA-matched sibling donor and 12 patients receiv-
ing an autologous (Auto) transplant. Also, data is given in Table 1.5
on 20 Hodgkin's lymphoma (HOD) patients, 5 receiving an allogeneic
(Allo) transplant from an HLA-matched sibling donor and 15 patients
receiving an autologous (Auto) transplant. Because there is a potential
for different efficacy of the two types of transplants for the two types
of lymphoma, a model with a main effect for type of transplant, a main
effect for disease type and an interactive term is of interest (coding
similar to 8.1b).

(a) Using a Weibull regression model, analyze this data by performing
a likelihood ratio global test of no effect of transplant type and
disease state on survival. Construct an ANOVA table to summarize
estimates of the risk coefficients and the results of the one degree
of freedom tests for each covariate in the model.

(b) Test the hypothesis of no disease-transplant type interaction using
a likelihood ratio test.

(c) Find point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for the relative
risk of death for an NHL Auto transplant patient as compared to an
NHL Allo transplant patient.

(d) Test the hypothesis that the death rates are the same for HOD Allo
transplants and NHL Allo patients. Repeat this test for Auto patients.

(e) Test the hypothesis that the death rates for Auto transplant and
Allo transplant patients are the same against the alternative they are
different for at least one disease group by a 2 degree of freedom test

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="422" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 12 Inference for Parametric Regression Models" -->

of Ho : h(t | NHL Allo) = h(t | NHL Auto) and $h \left( t | H O D \quad A l l o \right) =$
b(t | HOD Auto).

(f) Compare your results to those found in Exercise 3 of Chapter 8 by
using the semiparametric proportional hazards model.

12.4

test and in part d provide point and interval estimates of the acceleration
factor and the relative odds. Compare your results to those found in
Exercise 2.

12.5

factor and the relative odds. Compare your results to those found in

12.6

those found in that exercise. Estimate relative odds rather than relative
risks in part c.

12.7

variance-covariance matrix for the following models for each of the two
groups.

(a) A log normal model.

(b) A normal model.

(c) A generalized gamma model.

(d) Using the results of part c, test the hypothesis that $\theta = 0 .$ Interpret
your result in terms of model selection.

(e) Using the results of part c, test the hypothesis that $\theta = 1 .$ Interpret
your result in terms of model selection.

(f) Based on your results in this exercise and in Exercises 1 and 5,
which parametric model best fits the data for each of the two ploidy
groups?

and generalized gamma models.

<!-- PageBreak -->


## 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis


### 13.1 Introduction

In the previous chapters of this book, we have examined a variety of
techniques for analyzing survival data. With few exceptions, these tech-
niques are based on the assumption that the survival times of distinct
individuals are independent of each other. Although this assumption
may be valid in many experimental settings, it may be suspect in others.
For example, we may be making inferences about survival in a sample
of siblings or litter mates who share a common genetic makeup, or we
may be studying survival in a sample of married couples who share a
common, unmeasured, environment. A third example is where we are
studying the times to occurrence of different nonlethal diseases within
the same individual. In each of these situations, it is quite probable that
there is some association within groups of survival times in the sample.

A model that is becoming increasingly popular for modeling associ-
ation between individual survival times within subgroups is the use of
a frailty model. A frailty is an unobservable random effect shared by
subjects within a subgroup. This most common model for the frailty is a
common random effect that acts multiplicatively on the hazard rates of
all subgroup members. In this model, families with a large value of the
frailty will experience the event at earlier times then families with small

<!-- PageNumber="425" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="426" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->

values of the random effect. Thus the most "frail" individuals will die
early and late survivors will tend to come from more robust families.

Frailty models are also used in making adjustments for overdispersion
in univariate survival studies. Here, the frailty represents the total effect
on survival of the covariates not measured when collecting information
on individual subjects. If these effects are ignored, the resulting survival
estimates may be misleading. Corrections for this overdispersion allow
for adjustments for other unmeasured important effects.

The most common model for a frailty is the so-called shared frailty
model extension of the proportional hazards regression model. Here,
we assume that the hazard rate for the $j t h$ subject in the ith subgroup,
given the frailty, is of the form

(13.1.1)
$$b _ { i j } \left( t \right) = b _ { o } \left( t \right) \exp \left( \sigma w _ { i } + \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { \mathrm { i j } } \right) , i = 1 , \ldots , G , j = 1 , \ldots , n _ { i } ,$$

where $b _ { 0 } \left( t \right)$ is an arbitrary baseline hazard rate, $\mathrm { Z } _ { i j }$ is the vector of
covariates, $\beta$ the vector of regression coefficients, and $w _ { 1 } , \ldots , w _ { G }$ the
frailties. Here, we assume that the w's are an independent sample from
some distribution with mean 0 and variance 1. Note that, when $\sigma$
is zero, the model (13.1.1) reduces to the basic proportional hazards
model discussed in Chapter 8. The model allows for the sizes $n _ { i }$ of the
individual groups to differ from group to group and for the group sizes
to be 1. In some applications, it is more convenient to write the model
(13.1.1) as

(13.1.2)
$$b _ { i j } \left( t \right) = b _ { o } \left( t \right) u _ { i } \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { \mathrm { i j } } \right) , i = 1 , \ldots , G , j = 1 , \ldots , n _ { i } ,$$

where the ui's are an independent and identically distributed sample
from a distribution with mean 1 and some unknown variance. We see
clearly that, when nature picks a value of $u _ { i }$ greater than 1, individuals
within a given family tend to fail at a rate faster than under an inde-
pendence model where $u j$ is equal to 1 with probability 1. Conversely,
when $u _ { i }$ is less than 1, individuals in a family tend to have survival
longer than predicted under an independence model.

When we collect data, the $w _ { i }$ are not observable, so the joint dis-
tribution of the survival times of individuals within a group, found $\mathrm { b y }$
taking the expectation of $\exp \left[ - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { i j } \left( t \right) \right]$ with respect to $u _ { i }$ is given
by

$$S \left( x _ { i 1 } , \ldots , x _ { i n _ { i } } \right) = P \left[ X _ { i 1 } > x _ { i 1 } , \ldots , X _ { i n _ { i } } > x _ { i n _ { i } } \right]$$
$$= L P \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( x _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { \mathrm { i j } } \right) \right] .$$
(13.1.3)

Here, $L P \left( v \right) = E _ { U } \left[ \exp \left( - U v \right) \right]$ is the Laplace transform of the frailty $U .$
Common models proposed in the literature for the random effect are the
one-parameter gamma distribution (Clayton, 1978), the positive stable
distribution (Hougaard, 1986a), and the inverse Gaussian distribution
(Hougaard, 1986b). Other models for the frailty are the log normal

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="13.2 Score Test for Association 427" -->

distribution (McGilchrist and Aisbett, 1991), a power variance model
(Aalen, 1988; 1992), a uniform distribution (Lee and Klein, 1988) and a
threshold model (Lindley and Singpurwalla, 1986).

In this chapter, we shall examine estimation and inference procedures
for random effect models. In section 13.2, we will present a score test
of the hypothesis of no frailty effect $\left( \sigma = 0 \right)$ suggested by Commenges
and Andersen (1995). This test is valid for any frailty distribution and
provides an easy means of testing for a random effect in either the
multivariate $\mathrm { O T }$ univariate problem.

In sections 13.3, we present both a semiparametric and a parametric
approach to estimating the risk coefficients and the frailty parameter
for the most commonly used gamma frailty model. Here, estimating in
the semiparametric model involves implementing an EM (Expectation-
Maximization) algorithm. We give a series of references in the notes
showing where these techniques have been extended to other frailty
models.

In section 13.4, we present an alternative model for multivariate sur-
vival data suggested by Lee et al. (1992). For this approach, we assume
that the proportional hazards model holds marginally for each individ-
ual, but individuals within groups are associated. (Note that, for the
frailty approach, we assumed that the proportional hazards model is
true for each individual, conditional on the frailty.) Estimators of $\beta$
are the usual regression estimates, as discussed in Chapter 8. These
estimators are based on an "independence working model" and are
consistent for $\beta .$ The usual variance estimator, based on the observed
information matrix, however, is not correct when individuals are corre-
lated. An adjusted covariance matrix is used which is found by adjusting
the information matrix for the association between individuals.


### 13.2 Score Test for Association

To test for association between subgroups of individuals, a score test
has been developed by Commenges and Andersen (1995). The test
can be used to test for association, after an adjustment for covariate
effects has been made using Cox's proportional hazards model, or can
be applied when there are no covariates present. It can be used to test
for overdispersion in the univariate Cox proportional hazards model.
Although the test can be applied following a stratified Cox regression
model, for simplicity, we shall focus on the nonstratified model.

The test is based on the model (13.1.1). Here we have $G \mathrm { s u b g r o u p s }$
of subjects, and within the ith subgroup, we have $n _ { i }$ individuals. Note
that, for the univariate model, where we are testing for overdispersion,
$G$ is the total sample size and each $n _ { i }$ is 1. We are interested in testing
the null hypothesis that $\sigma$ is equal to zero against the alternative that $\sigma$ is

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="428" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->

not zero. No distributional assumptions are made about the distribution
of the random effects $w _ { i } .$

The data we need to construct the test statistics consists of the on
study time $T _ { i j } ,$ the event indicator $\delta _ { i j } ,$ and the covariate vector $\mathrm { Z } _ { i j }$ for
the jth observation in the ith group. Using this data, we can construct
the "at risk" indicator $Y _ { i j } \left( t \right)$ at time $t$ for an observation. Recall that $Y _ { i j } \left( t \right)$
is 1 when the ijth individual is at risk. Then, we fit the $\mathrm { C o x }$ proportional
hazards model, $b \left( t | Z _ { i j } \right) = b _ { o } \left( t \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right) ,$ and we obtain the partial
maximum likelihood estimates $\mathrm { b }$ of $\beta$ and the Breslow estimate $\widehat { H } _ { o } \left( t \right)$
of the cumulative baseline hazard rate (see Chapter 8). Let

$$S ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( t \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } Y _ { i j } \left( t \right) \exp \left( \mathrm { b } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) .$$
(13.2.1)

Note that $S ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( t \right)$ is simply the sum of $\exp \left( \mathrm { b } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right)$ over all individuals at
risk at time $t .$ Finally, we need to compute $M _ { i j } = \delta _ { i j } - \widehat { H } _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( b ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right) ,$
the martingale residual for the ijth individual (see section 11.3).

The score statistic is given by

$$T = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \left\{ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } M _ { i j } \right\} ^ { 2 } - D + C ,$$
(13.2.2)

where $D$ is the total number of deaths and

$$C = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \frac { \delta _ { i j } } { S ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( T _ { i j } \right) ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { b = 1 } ^ { G } \left[ \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } Y _ { b k } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \mathrm { b } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { b k } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } .$$
(13.2.3)

The test statistic can also be written as

$$T = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n _ { j } } M _ { i j } M _ { i k } + \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } M _ { i j } ^ { 2 } - N \right) + C ,$$
(13.2.4)

where $N$ is the total sample size.

Here, we see that the score statistic is the sum of three terms. The
first is the sum of the pairwise correlations between individuals in the
subgroups, the second a measure of overdispersion, and the third a
correction term $C$ which tends to zero as $N$ increases.

To estimate the variance of T let $\widehat { \Sigma }$ be the estimated variance-
covariance matrix of $\mathrm { b }$ with elements $\widehat { \sigma } _ { b k } ,$ $b = 1 , \ldots , p$ $k = 1 , \ldots , p .$
Let

$$p _ { i j } \left( t \right) = \frac { Y _ { i j } \left( t \right) \exp \left( b ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right) } { S ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( t \right) }$$

and

$$\bar { p } _ { i } \left( t \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } p _ { i j } \left( t \right) , i = 1 , \ldots , G .$$
(13.2.5)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="429" -->
<!-- PageHeader="13.2 Score Test for Association" -->

For each distinct death time, $0 = t _ { o } < t _ { 1 } < \ldots < t _ { d } ,$ let $d _ { k }$ be $\mathrm { t h e }$
observed number of deaths at time $t _ { k } .$ Recall that the martingale residual
for the jth observation in the ith group at time $t _ { k }$ is given by

$$M _ { i j } \left( t _ { k } \right) = \left\{ \begin{array}{} \delta _ { i j } - H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) , \exp \left( \mathrm { b } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) & \mathrm { i f } T _ { i j } \leq t _ { k } , \\ - H _ { o } \left( t _ { k } \right) \exp \left( \mathrm { b } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) & \mathrm { i f } T _ { i j } > t _ { k } . \end{array} \right.$$
(13.2.6)

Let $\bar { M } _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } M _ { i j } \left( t _ { k } \right)$ and $\bar { M } _ { i } \left( t _ { o } \right) = 0 .$ For each group, compute

$$Q _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) = 2 \left[ \bar { M } _ { i } \left( t _ { k - 1 } \right) - \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { G } \bar { M } _ { g } \left( t _ { k - 1 } \right) \bar { p } _ { g } \left( t _ { k } \right) - \bar { p } _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) + \sum _ { g = 1 } ^ { G } \bar { p } _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] , .$$
$$k = 1 , \ldots , d .$$
(13.2.7)

Finally, for each of the p-covariates, compute

$$\theta _ { b } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { d } Q _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) d _ { k } \left\{ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } p _ { i j } \left( t _ { k } \right) Z _ { i j b } \right\} , b = 1 , \ldots , p .$$
(13.2.8)

The estimated variance of the score statistic is given by

$$V = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { d } Q _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) ^ { 2 } \bar { p } _ { i } \left( t _ { k } \right) d _ { k } + \sum _ { b = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } \theta _ { b } \theta _ { k } \widehat { \sigma } _ { b k } .$$
(13.2.9)

The test statistic for the test of no association is given by $T / V ^ { 1 / 2 }$ which
has an asymptotic normal distribution.

Mantel et al. (1977) reports the results of a litter-matched study of the
tumorigenesis of a drug. In the experiment, rats were taken from fifty
distinct litters, and one rat of the litter was randomly selected and given
the drug. For each litter, two rats were selected as controls and were
given a placebo. All mice were females. Possible associations between
litter mates in their times to development of tumors may be due to
common genetic backgrounds shared by siblings. We shall test this
hypothesis using the score test for association. The data is given in
Table 13.1.

To perform the test, we first fit a Cox proportional hazards regression
model to the data ignoring possible dependence between litter mates.
A single covariate was used, which has the value 1 if the rat was in the
treated group and 0 if the rat was in the placebo group. The estimate of
$\beta$ is $b = 0 . 8 9 7 5$ with an estimated variance of 0.1007 using Breslow's
likelihood for ties. The value of $T$ computed from (13.2.4) is 8.91, and
$V = 4 5 . 0 3 .$ The standardized test statistic is $8 . 9 1 / \sqrt { 4 5 . 0 3 } = 1 . 3 3$ which
has a p-value of 0.184 from the standard normal table. This suggests
that there is no evidence of a litter effect in this experiment.

EXAMPLE 13.1

<!-- PageNumber="430" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->

<td>$8 3 ^ { + } . 7 7 ^ { + }$</td>
</tr>

\+ Censored observation


### 13.3 Estimation for the Gamma Frailty Model

In this section we present a scheme for estimating the risk coefficients,
baseline hazard rate, and frailty parameter for a frailty model based on
a gamma distributed frailty. For this model, we assume that, for the
jth individual in the ith group, the hazard rate given the unobserv-
able frailty $u _ { i }$ is of the form (13.1.2) with the $u _ { i }$ an independent and

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="431" -->
<!-- PageHeader="13.3 Estimation for the Gamma Frailty Model" -->

identically distributed sample of gamma random variables with density
function,

$$g \left( u \right) = \frac { u ^ { \left( 1 / \theta - 1 \right) } \exp \left( - u / \theta \right) } { \Gamma \left[ 1 / \theta \right] \theta ^ { 1 / \theta } } .$$
(13.3.1)

With this frailty distribution, the mean of $U$ is 1 and the variance is 0, so
that large values of $\theta$ reflect a greater degree of heterogeneity among
groups and $a$ stronger association within groups. The joint survival
function for the $n _ { i }$ individuals within the ith group is given by

$$S \left\{ x _ { i 1 } , \ldots , x _ { i n _ { i } } \right\} = P \left[ X _ { i 1 } > x _ { i 1 } , \ldots , X _ { i n _ { i } } > x _ { i n _ { i } } \right]$$
$$= \left[ 1 + \theta \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( x _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right] ^ { - 1 / \theta } .$$

The association between group members as measured by Kendall's $\tau$ is
$\theta / \left( \theta + 2 \right) ,$ and $\theta = 0$ corresponds to the case of independence.

Estimation for this model is based on the log likelihood function. Our
data consists of the usual triple $\left( T _ { i j } , \delta _ { i j } , Z _ { i j } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , G ,$ $j = 1 , \ldots , n _ { i } .$
Let $D _ { i } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \delta _ { i j }$ be the number of events in the ith group. Then, the
observable log likelihood is given by

$$L \left( \theta , B \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } D _ { i } \ln \theta - \ln \left[ \Gamma \left( 1 / \theta \right) \right] + \ln \left[ \Gamma \left( 1 / \theta + D _ { i } \right) \right]$$
$$- \left( 1 / \theta + D _ { i } \right) \ln \left[ 1 + \theta \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right]$$
$$+ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \delta _ { i j } \left\{ \beta ^ { t } Z _ { i j } + \ln \left[ b _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \right] \right\} .$$
(13.3.2)

If one assumes a parametric form for $b _ { o } \left( \right) ,$ then, maximum likelihood
estimates are available by directly maximizing $\left( 1 3 . 3 . 2 \right) .$ Estimates of
the variability of the parameter estimates are obtained by inverting the
information matrix.

If a parametric form is not assumed for $b _ { 0 } \left( \right) ,$ the semiparametric
estimates are obtained by using an EM algorithm. Here, we consider
the full likelihood we would have if the frailties were observed. This
log likelihood is given by

$$L _ { \text { FUL } } = L _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) + L _ { 2 } \left( \beta , H _ { 0 } \right) ,$$

where

$$L _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) = - G \left[ \left( 1 / \theta \right) \ln \theta + \ln \Gamma \left[ 1 / \theta \right] \right] + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \left[ 1 / \theta + D _ { i } - 1 \right] \ln u _ { i } - u _ { i } / \theta$$
(13.3.3)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="432" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->

and

$$L _ { 2 } \left( \beta , H _ { 0 } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \delta _ { i j } \left[ \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } + \ln b _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \right] - u _ { i } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) .$$
(13.3.4)

The EM algorithm provides a means of maximizing complex likeli-
hoods. In the E (or Estimation) step of the algorithm the expected value
of $L _ { \mathrm { F U L } }$ is computed, given the current estimates of the parameters and
the observable data. In the M (or maximization) step of the algorithm,
estimates of the parameters which maximize the expected value of $L _ { \mathrm { F I I I } }$
from the $E$ step are obtained. The algorithm iterates between these two
steps until convergence.

To apply the E-step to our problem, we use the fact that, given the
data and the current estimates of the parameters, the ui's are indepen-
dent gamma random variables with shape parameters $A _ { i } = \left[ 1 / \theta + D _ { i } \right]$
and scale parameters $C _ { i } = \left[ 1 / \theta + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \bar { H _ { o } } \left( \bar { T _ { i j } } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right] .$ Thus,

$$E \left[ u _ { i } | \text { Data } \right] = \frac { A _ { i } } { C _ { i } } \text { and } E \left[ \ln u _ { i } \right] = \left[ \psi \left( A _ { i } \right) - \ln C _ { i } \right] ,$$
(13.3.5)

where $\psi \left( . \right)$ is the digamma function. Substituting these values in (13.3.3)
and (13.3.4) completes the E-step of the algorithm.

For the M-step, note that $E \left[ L _ { 2 } \left( \beta , H _ { o } \right) \right.$ | Data] is expressed as

$$E \left[ L _ { 2 } \left( \beta , H _ { 0 } \right) | \mathrm { D a t a } \right] = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \delta _ { i j } \left[ \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } + \ln b _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \right] - \frac { A _ { i } } { C _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right)$$
(13.3.6)

which depends on the nuisance parameter $b _ { 0 } \left( \right) .$ This likelihood is of
the form of the complete likelihood from which the partial likelihood
is constructed for the Cox model with the addition of a group specific
covariate, $\ln \left( A _ { i } / C _ { i } \right) ,$ with a known risk coefficient of 1. If we let $t _ { \left( k \right) }$
be the kth smallest death time, regardless of subgroup, and $m _ { \left( k \right) }$ the
number of deaths at time $t _ { \left( k \right) } ,$ for $k = 1 , \ldots , D$ and if we denote by $\widehat { u } _ { b }$
and $\mathrm { Z } _ { b } ,$ the expected value of the frailty and the covariate vector for the
$b t h$ individual, then, the partial likelihood to be maximized in the $M$
step is

$$L _ { 3 } \left( \beta \right) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { D } \left\{ S _ { \left( k \right) } - m _ { \left( k \right) } \ln \left[ \sum _ { b \in R \left( T _ { \left( k \right) } \right) } \widehat { u } _ { b } \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { b } \right) \right] \right\} ,$$
(13.3.7)

where $S _ { \left( k \right) }$ is the sum of the covariates of individuals who died at time
$t _ { \left( k \right) } .$ Note the similarity of this partial likelihood to the Breslow likelihood
for the usual Cox regression model. An estimate of the $H _ { o } \left( t \right)$ from this
step is given by

$$\widehat { H } _ { o } \left( t \right) = \sum _ { t _ { \left( k \right) } \leq t } b _ { k o }$$
(13.3.8)

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="433" -->
<!-- PageHeader="13.3 Estimation for the Gamma Frailty Model" -->

where

$$b _ { k o } = \frac { m _ { \left( k \right) } } { \sum _ { b \in R \left( t _ { \left( k \right) } \right) } \widehat { u } _ { b } \text { exp } \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { b } \right) } .$$

A full implementation of the EM algorithm is, then,

Step 0. Provide initial estimates of $\beta ,$ $\theta$ and thus $b _ { k o } ,$ $k = 1 , . . , d .$

Step 1. (E step). Compute $A _ { i } ,$ $C _ { i } ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , G$ and $\widehat { u } _ { b } ,$ $b = 1 , \ldots , n$
based on the current values of the parameters.

Step 2. (M step). Update the estimate of $\beta$ (and the $b _ { k o }$ using the partial
likelihood in (13.3.7). Update the estimate of $\theta$ based on the
likelihood $L _ { 4 } = E \left[ L _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) \right.$ | Data] given by

$$L _ { 4 } = - G \left[ \left( 1 / \theta \right) \ln \theta + \ln \Gamma \left( 1 / \theta \right) \right] + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \left[ 1 / \theta + D _ { i } - 1 \right] \left[ \psi \left( A _ { i } \right) - \ln C _ { i } \right] - \frac { A _ { i } } { \theta C _ { i } }$$

Step 3. Iterate between Steps 1 and 2 until convergence.

Although this algorithm eventually converges to the maximum likeli-
hood estimates of $\theta ,$ $\beta ,$ and $H _ { o } ,$ the rate of convergence is quite slow.
A quicker algorithm, suggested by Nielsen et al.(1992), is a modified
profile likelihood EM algorithm. Here we use a grid of possible values
for the frailty parameter $\theta .$ For each fixed value of $\theta ,$ the following EM
algorithm is used to obtain an estimate of $\beta _ { \theta } .$

Step 0. Provide an initial estimate of $\beta$ (and hence $b _ { k o }$

Step 1. (E step). Compute $\widehat { u } _ { h } ,$ $b = 1 , \ldots , n$ based on the current values
of the parameters.

Step 2. (M step). Update the estimate of $\beta _ { \theta }$ (and the $b _ { k o }$ using the
partial likelihood in (13.3.7).

Step 3. Iterate between steps 1 and 2 until convergence.

The value of the profile likelihood for this value of $\theta$ is, then, given by
$L \left( \theta , \beta _ { \theta } \right)$ using (13.3.2). The value of $\theta$ which maximizes this quantity is,
then, the maximum likelihood estimate. Both approaches will converge
to the same estimates.

The standard errors of the estimates of $\beta ,$ $\theta$ and $b _ { k o }$ are based on the
inverse of the observed information matrix constructed by taking partial
derivatives of minus the observable likelihood (13.3.2). The information
matrix is a square matrix of size $D + p + 1 .$ The elements of this matrix
are as follows:

$$\left. \frac { - \partial ^ { 2 } L } { \partial b _ { k o } \partial b _ { j 0 } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } - \left( D _ { i } + 1 / \theta \right) \frac { Q _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( \beta , t _ { \left( k \right) } \right) Q _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( \beta , t _ { \left( j \right) } \right) } { \left[ 1 / \theta + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right. } \right] ^ { 2 }$$
$$+ I \left( k = j \right) \frac { m _ { j } } { \left( b _ { j o } \right) ^ { 2 } } , j , k = 1 , \ldots , D ,$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="434" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->

$$\frac { - \partial ^ { 2 } L } { \partial b _ { k o } \partial \beta _ { v } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \frac { D _ { i } + 1 / \theta } { \left[ 1 / \theta + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right) ^ { 2 } \right. }$$
$$\times \left\{ \left[ 1 / \theta + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right] Q _ { i v } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \left( \beta , t _ { \left( k \right) } \right) \right.$$
$$\left. - Q _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( \beta , t _ { \langle k \rangle } \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } Z _ { i j v } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right\} ,$$
$$k = 1 , \ldots , D , v = 1 , \ldots , D ,$$

$$\frac { - \partial ^ { 2 } L } { \partial b _ { k o } \partial \theta } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } Q _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( \beta , t _ { \left( k \right) } \right) \left\{ \frac { D _ { i } - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) } { 1 + \theta \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) } \right\} , k = 1 , \ldots , L$$

$$\frac { - \partial ^ { 2 } L } { \partial \beta _ { v } \partial \beta _ { w } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \frac { D _ { i } + 1 / \theta } { 1 / \theta + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { 0 } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right) }$$
$$\times \left\{ \left[ 1 / \theta + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right] \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) Z _ { i j v } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j w } \mathrm { e x p } \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right.$$
$$\left. - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) Z _ { i j v } \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) Z _ { i j w } \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right\} ,$$
$$w = 1 , \ldots , p$$
v,

$$\left. \frac { - \partial ^ { 2 } L } { \partial \beta _ { v } \partial \theta } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \frac { \left[ D _ { i } - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \mathrm { e x p } \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right] \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) Z _ { i j v } \mathrm { e x p } \left( \mathrm { \beta } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) } { \left[ 1 + \theta \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \mathrm { g } ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right. } \right] ^ { 2 }$$

,
$v = 1 , \ldots , p ,$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="435" -->
<!-- PageHeader="13.3 Estimation for the Gamma Frailty Model" -->

and

$$\frac { - \partial ^ { 2 } L } { \partial \theta ^ { 2 } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } \left\{ \sum _ { r = 0 } ^ { D _ { i } - 1 } - \frac { 1 + 2 r \theta } { \left( r \theta ^ { 2 } + \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 \ln \theta - 3 } { \theta ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 2 } { \theta ^ { 3 } } \ln \left[ 1 / \theta + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { o } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \right] \right.$$
$$\begin{array}{} { 3 / \theta + D _ { i } + 2 \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } H _ { 0 } \left( T _ { i j } \right) e x p \left( \beta ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right) \right] \left[ 2 + \theta + D _ { i } \right] } \\ { + \frac { t } { T } } \end{array}$$
0+ 02
H.(Tij) exp{B'Zij}

j=1

where

$$Q _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } \left( \beta , t \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } { } ^ { * } \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) , \quad Q _ { i v } ^ { \left( 1 \right) } \left( \beta , t \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } Z _ { i j v } \exp \left( \beta ^ { \mathrm { t } } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) \mathrm { a n d }$$
$$Q _ { i v w } ^ { \left( 2 \right) } \left( \beta , t \right) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } Z _ { i j v } Z _ { i j w } \exp \left\{ \beta ^ { t } Z _ { i j } \right\} ,$$

with each of these starred sums only over those at risk at time t.


#### EXAMPLE 13.1

(continued): We shall fit the gamma frailty model to the litter data
presented in Example 13.1. Here, we have a single covariate $Z$ denoting
whether the rat was fed the drug or placebo. Each litter defines a group.
Applying the EM algorithm and after computing the information matrix,
we find the following results:


<table>
<tr>
<th>Model</th>
<th>Treatment</th>
<th>Frailty</th>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Cox Model (Frailty)</td>
<td>$b = 0 . 9 0 4 ,$ $S E \left( b \right) = 0 . 3 2 3$</td>
<td>$\widehat { \theta } = 0 . 4 7 2 ,$ $S E \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) = 0 . 4 6 2$</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Cox Model (Independence)</td>
<td>$b = 0 . 8 9 7 ,$ $S E \left( b \right) = 0 . 3 1 7$</td>
<td>—</td>
</tr>
</table>


Note that, when the frailty is ignored, the estimate of $\beta$ is closer to
zero and the estimated standard error is reduced. This is a consequence
of the frailty model. In this example, we also see that there is no
significant random effect because the Wald statistic $\widehat { \theta } / S E \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) = 1 . 0 2$
which gives a p-value of 0.31. The estimated value of Kendall's $\tau$ is
$\widehat { \theta } / \left( 2 + \widehat { \theta } \right) = 0 . 1 9$ with a standard error of $2 S E \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) / \left( 2 + \widehat { \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } = 0 . 4 3 .$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="36 Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->


#### Practical Notes

1\. A SAS macro is available at our website for implementing these
techniques. They are quite computer intensive.

2\. Similar estimating schemes have been developed for the positive
stable frailty (Wang et al. 1995) and for the inverse gaussian frailty
(Klein et al. 1992).

3\. The estimating scheme can be simplified if a parametric model is
assumed for the baseline hazard rate. See Costigan and Klein (1993)
for a survey of this approach.

4\. Additional models and examples are found in Hougaard (2001).


#### Theoretical Notes

1\. Estimation for the semiparametric gamma frailty model was first con-
sidered by Clayton (1978) and Clayton and Cuzick (1985). Based on
a suggestion of Gill (1985), the EM algorithm was developed inde-
pendently by Klein (1992) and Nielsen et al. (1992).

2\. Neilsen et al. (1992) give a counting process derivation of this ap-
proach to estimation.

3\. The calculation of the variance-covariance matrix given here was
introduced in Andersen et al. (1997). This is a nonstandard ap-
proach but there is good empirical data and some Monte Carlo work
(Morsing 1994) which suggests that it is valid. It is of interest to note
that, if one uses the same approach to derive a variance estimate
for the usual Cox model, then, the inverse of the information matrix
gives precisely the common formula for the variance.


### 13.4 Marginal Model for Multivariate Survival

In the previous section, we modeled multivariate survival data by a
conditional proportional hazards model given the unobserved frailty.
With this model, the marginal distributions no longer followed a
simple Cox model. In fact, except for the positive stable frailty
model, the marginal distributions do not follow a proportional haz-
ards model.

An alternative model for multivariate survival data has been suggested
by Lee et al. (1992). In this approach, a proportional hazards model is
assumed marginally for each individual, that is, for the jth individual in
the ith group, the marginal hazard rate given an individual's covariates

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="437" -->
<!-- PageHeader="13.4 Marginal Model for Multivariate Survival" -->

$\mathrm { Z } _ { i j }$ is expressed by

(13.4.1)
$$b _ { i j } \left( t | \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) = b _ { o } \left( t \right) \exp \left( \beta ^ { t } \mathrm { Z } _ { i j } \right) , j = 1 , \ldots , n _ { i } , i = 1 , \ldots , G .$$

As in previous sections of this chapter, we allow the individual obser-
vations within each of the $G$ groups to be associated.

To estimate $\beta ,$ we proceed with an "independence working model"
for the data. We pretend that all observations are independent of
each other and construct the partial likelihood function for a sample
of $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { G } n _ { i }$ observations, as in Chapter 8. Using this partial likelihood
function, $\mathrm { b } ,$ the estimator of $\beta$ is found. Lee et al. (1992) show that this
estimator is consistent for $\beta ,$ provided the marginal model $\left( 1 3 . 4 . 1 \right)$ is
correctly specified. However, the information matrix obtained from this
likelihood does not provide a valid estimator of the variance-covariance
matrix of $\mathrm { b } .$

To estimate the variance of $b ,$ a "sandwich" estimator is used. This
estimator adjusts the usual covariance matrix for the possible association
between the event times within groups. To construct the covariance
matrix, let $\widehat { \mathrm { V } }$ be the usual $p \times p$ matrix for $\mathrm { b } ,$ based on the
independence working model. $S _ { i j }$ be the score residual for $Z _ { i j k }$
for the jth member of the ith group, $i = 1 , \ldots , G ,$ $j = 1 , \ldots , n _ { i } ,$ $k =$
$1 , \ldots p .$ Recall, as defined in (11.6.1), the score residual for the kth
covariate is

$$S _ { i j k } = \delta _ { i j } \left[ Z _ { i j k } - \bar { Z } _ { k } \left( T _ { i j } \right) \right] - \sum _ { t _ { b } \leq T _ { i j } } \left[ Z _ { i j k } - \bar { Z } _ { k } \left( t _ { b } \right) \right] d \bar { H } _ { 0 } \left( t _ { b } \right) ,$$
(13.4.2)

where $\bar { Z } _ { k } \left( t \right)$ is the average value of the kth covariate, $t _ { 1 } , \ldots t _ { D }$ are the
observed death times, and $d \bar { H } _ { 0 } \left( t _ { b } \right)$ is the jump in the estimate of the
baseline rate at time $t _ { b } .$ Summing these values, we compute the $p \times p$
matrix $\mathrm { C }$ defined by

$$C _ { b , k } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { g } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { n _ { i } } S _ { i j b } S _ { i m k }$$
(13.4.3)

The adjusted estimator of the variance of b is given by

$$\widetilde { \mathrm { V } } = \widehat { \mathrm { V } } \mathrm { C } \widehat { \mathrm { V } } .$$

The estimator $\mathrm { b }$ has a large sample p-variate normal distribution with
a mean of $\beta$ and a variance estimated by $\widetilde { \mathrm { V } } ,$ so the global and local
Wald tests discussed in Chapter 8 can be constructed. Note that, in this
model, we have no estimate of the strength of association between
individuals within groups. We simply have an estimator of $\beta$ and an
adjusted estimator of its standard error.

EXAMPLE 13.1
(continued): We shall fit the marginal model to the litter data pre-
sented in Example 13.1. Here, we have a single covariate $Z$ denoting
whether the rat was fed the drug or placebo. Each litter defines a group.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="438" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Chapter 13 Multivariate Survival Analysis" -->

Fitting a Cox regression model, we obtain $b = 0 . 8 9 7$ and $\widehat { V } = 0 . 1 0 0 7 .$
Computing (13.4.3), we find $C = 8 . 8 9 3$ so $\widetilde { V } = 0 . 1 0 0 7 ^ { 2 } \left( 8 . 8 9 3 \right) = 0 . 0 9 0 2$
and the standard error of $b$ is $\sqrt { 0 . 0 9 0 2 } = 0 . 3 0 0 0 .$ A 95% confidence in-
terval for the relative risk of death for a rat given the drug compared to
a placebo-fed rat is $\exp \left[ 0 . 8 9 7 \pm 1 . 9 6 \left( 0 . 3 0 0 0 \right) \right] = \left( 1 . 3 6 , 4 . 4 2 \right) .$ Note that,
if the naive estimate of the standard error $\sqrt { 0 . 1 0 0 7 } = 0 . 3 1 7 3$ were used,
the 95% confidence interval for the relative risk would be (1.32, 4.57),
which is slightly wider than that obtained using the adjusted variance.


#### Practical Notes

1\. A FORTRAN program to compute the adjusted variance estimator
can be found in Lin (1993). This variance estimator is also available
in S-Plus.

2\. When the event times within groups are independent, the adjusted
variance estimator reduces to a robust variance estimator for the Cox
regression model proposed by Lin and Wei (1989).

3\. If the baseline hazard rate is assumed to be different for each group,
but a common $\beta$ acts on these rates, then, Wei et al. (1989) provide a
similar approach based on an independence working model with a
sandwich estimator for the variance. Details and examples are found
in Lin (1993).

4\. Score residuals are available in SAS.


### 13.5 Exercises

13.1
Batchelor and Hackett (1970) have reported the results of a study of 16
acutely burned patients treated with skin allografts. Patients received
from one to four grafts. For each graft, the time in days to rejection of
the graft was recorded as well as an indicator variable Z which had a
value of 1 if the graft was a good match of HLA skin type and 0 if it
was a poor match. The survival times of some grafts were censored by
the death of the patient. The data is recorded below.

The survival time of an allograft is thought to depend on the degree of
HLA matching between the patient and the donor and on the strength of
the patient's immune response. Test the hypothesis of a random patient
effect due to differing immune responses by applying the score test for
association.

13.2
McGilchrist and Aisbett (1991) report data on the recurrence times of
infections of 38 kidney patients using a portable dialysis machine. For
each patient, two times to recurrence of an infection at the site of
insertion of the catheter placement (in days), $\left( T _ { 1 } , T _ { 2 } \right) ,$ are recorded as

GN; $Z _ { 4 } ,$ indicator of whether the patient had disease type AN; and $Z _ { 5 } ,$
indicator of whether the patient had disease type PKD. The data is
recorded below.

13.3
(a) Using the data on skin grafts in Exercise 1, fit a standard propor-
tional hazards model with a single covariate reflecting the degree
of matching, ignoring the information on which patient the graft
was applied.

(b) Fit the semiparametric gamma frailty model to this data. As an initial
guess of the frailty parameter, use a value of 0.55. Find the estimate
of the regression coefficient and its standard error. Compare this
value to that found in part a. Test the hypothesis of no association
in this model.

13.4
(a) Using the data in Exercise 2 and ignoring any patient effect, fit a
proportional hazards model to the times to infection using the five
covariates $Z _ { 1 } , \ldots , Z _ { 5 } .$ Construct an ANOVA table for your estimates.

(b) Using the marginal model with the corrected variance estimators,
repeat part a. Compare the variance estimates in the two models.

13.5
(a) Using the data in Exercise 1, fit a Cox model, ignoring any patient
effect.

<!-- PageNumber="441" -->
<!-- PageHeader="13.5 Exercises" -->

(b) Fit the marginal model to this data and compute the corrected
variance estimate. Using the corrected variance estimate, perform
the Wald test of the hypothesis of no effect of degree of matching
on graft survival. Compare the results of this test to a similar test
based on the results of part a.

</figure>


## A Numerical Techniques for Maximization

Many of the procedures discussed in this volume require maximizing
the log likelihood or partial log likelihood function. For many models,
it is impossible to perform this maximization analytically, so, numerical
methods must be employed. In this appendix, we shall summarize some
techniques which can be used in both univariate and multivariate cases.
The reader is referred to a text on statistical computing, such as Thisted
(1988), for a more detailed discussion of these techniques.


### A.1 Univariate Methods

Suppose we wish to find the value $x$ which maximizes a function $f \left( \right)$ of
a single variable. Under some mild regularity conditions, $x$ maximizes
$f$ if the score equation $f ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ equals 0 and $f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( x \right) < 0 .$ We present
three numerical methods which attempt to find the maximum of $f \left( \right)$
by solving the score equation. Some care must be taken when using
these routines because they do not ensure that the second derivative of
$f$ is negative at the solution we find.

The first technique is the bisection method. Here, the algorithm starts
with two initial values, $\mathcal{X} _ { I }$ and $\mathcal{X} _ { U } ,$ which bracket the root of $f ^ { \prime } \left( x \right) = 0 ,$
that is, $f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { L } \right) \cdot f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { U } \right) < 0 .$ A new guess at the root is taken to be the

<!-- PageNumber="443" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="444 Appendix A Numerical Techniques for Maximization" -->

midpoint of the interval $\left( x _ { L } , x _ { U } \right) ,$ namely, $x _ { N } = \left( x _ { L } + x _ { U } \right) / 2 .$ If $f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { L } \right)$
and $f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { N } \right)$ have the same sign, $\mathcal{X} _ { \mathrm { I } }$ is replaced by $x _ { \mathrm { N } } ,$ otherwise $\mathcal{X} _ { L }$ is
replaced by $\mathcal{X} _ { \mathrm { N } } .$ In either case, the algorithm continues with the new
values of $X _ { \mathrm { L } }$ and $\mathcal{X} _ { U }$ until the desired accuracy is achieved. At each step,
the length of the interval $\left( x _ { U } - x _ { L } \right)$ is a measure of the largest possible
difference between our updated guess at the root of $f ^ { \prime } \left( \right)$ and the actual
value of the root.

A second method, of use when one has good initial starting values
and a complicated second derivative of $f \left( \right) ,$ is the secant method or
regula falsi. Again, we start with two initial guesses at the root, $\mathcal{X} _ { o } .$
and $\mathcal{X} _ { 1 } .$ These guesses need not bracket the root. After $i$ steps of the
algorithm, the new guess at the root of $f ^ { \prime } \left( x \right)$ is given by

$$x _ { i + 1 } = x _ { i } - f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { i } \right) \left( x _ { i } - x _ { i - 1 } \right) / \left[ f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { i } \right) - f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { i - 1 } \right) \right] .$$
(A.1)

Iterations continue until convergence. Typical stopping criteria $a r e$

$$| x _ { i + 1 } - x _ { i } | < \gamma , | f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { i + 1 } \right) | < \gamma$$

or

$$| \left( x _ { i + 1 } - x _ { i } \right) / x _ { i } | < \gamma ,$$

where $\gamma$ is some small number.

The third method is the Newton-Raphson technique. Here, a single
initial guess, $\mathcal{X} _ { o } ,$ of the root is made. After i steps of the algorithm, the
updated guess is given by

$$x _ { i + 1 } = x _ { i } - f ^ { \prime } \left( x _ { i } \right) / f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( x _ { i } \right) .$$
(A.2)

Again, the iterative procedure continues until the desired level of $a c -$
curacy is met. Compared to the secant method, this technique has
the advantage of requiring a single starting value, and convergence is
quicker than the secant method when the starting values are good. Both
the secant and Newton-Raphson techniques may fail to converge when
the starting values are not close to the maximum.

Suppose we have the following 10 uncensored observations from a
Weibull model with scale parameter $\lambda = 1$ and shape parameter «, that
is, $b \left( t \right) = \alpha t ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - t ^ { \alpha } } .$

Data: $2 . 5 7 , 0 . 5 8 , 0 . 8 2 , 1 . 0 2 , 0 . 7 8 , 0 . 4 6 , 1 . 0 4 , 0 . 4 3 , 0 . 6 9 , 1 . 3 7$

To find the maximum likelihood estimator of $\alpha ,$ we need to maximize
the log likelihood $f \left( \alpha \right) = \ln L \left( \alpha \right) = n \ln \left( \alpha \right) + \left( \alpha - 1 \right) \sum \ln \left( t _ { j } \right) - \sum t _ { j } ^ { \alpha } .$
Here, $f ^ { \prime } \left( \alpha \right) = n / \alpha + \sum \ln \left( t _ { j } \right) - \sum t _ { j } ^ { \alpha } \ln \left( t _ { j } \right) ,$ and $f ^ { \prime \prime } \left( \alpha \right) = - n / \alpha ^ { 2 } -$
$\sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \left[ \ln \left( t _ { j } \right) \right] ^ { 2 } .$

Applying the bisection method with $\alpha _ { L } = 1 . 5$ and $\alpha _ { U } = 2$ and
stopping the algorithm when $| f ^ { \prime } \left( \alpha \right) | < 0 . 0 1 ,$ we have the following
values:

EXAMPLE A.1

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="445" -->
<!-- PageHeader="A.2 .2 Multivariate Methods" -->


<table>

### A.2 Multivariate Methods

We present three methods to maximize a function of more than one
variable. The first is the method of steepest ascent which requires only
the vector of first derivatives of the function. This method is robust
to the starting values used in the iterative scheme, but may require a
large number of steps to converge to the maximum. The second is the
multivariate extension of the Newton-Raphson method. This method,
which requires both the first and second derivatives of the function,

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="446" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix A Numerical Techniques for Maximization" -->

converges quite rapidly when the starting values are close to the root,
but may not converge when the starting values are poorly chosen. The
third, called Marquardt's (1963) method, is a compromise between these
two methods. It uses a blending constant which controls how closely
the algorithm resembles either the method of steepest ascent or the
Newton-Raphson method.

Some notation is needed before presenting the three methods. Let
$f \left( x \right)$ be a function of the p-dimensional vector $\mathrm { x } = \left( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p } \right) ^ { t } .$ Let
☒

☒ ☒
$\mathrm { u } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ be the p-vector of first order partial derivatives of $f \left( x \right) ,$ that is,
☒

$$\mathrm { u } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \left[ u _ { 1 } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) , \ldots , u _ { p } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right] ^ { t } ,$$ ☒ ☒ ☒
(A.3)

where

$$u _ { j } \left( x \right) = \frac { \partial f \left( x \right) } { \partial x _ { j } } , j = 1 , \ldots , p .$$ ☒

Let $\mathrm { H } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ be the $p \times p$ Hessian matrix of mixed second partial derivatives
☒

of $f \left( x \right) ,$ defined by
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { H } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \left( H _ { i j } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) \right) , i , j = 1 , \ldots , p \mathrm { w h e r e } H _ { i j } \left( \mathrm { x } \right) = \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } f \left( \mathrm { x } \right) } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { i } } .$$ ☒ ☒ ☒ ☒
(A.4)
.

The method of steepest ascent starts with $\mathrm { a n }$ initial guess, $\mathrm { X } _ { o } ,$ of
the point which maximizes $f \left( \mathrm { x } \right) .$ At any point, the gradient vector
☒ ☒
$\mathrm { u } \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ points the direction of steepest ascent of the function $f \left( x \right) .$ The
☒

☒ ☒
algorithm moves along this direction by an amount $d \quad t o \quad a$ new estimate
of the maximum from the current estimate. The step size $d$ is chosen to
maximize the function in this direction, that is, we pick $d$ to maximize
$f \left[ x _ { k } + d u \left( x _ { k } \right) \right.$ This requires maximizing a function of a single variable,
☒

so that any of the techniques discussed earlier can be employed.

The updated guess at the point which maximizes $f \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is given by
☒ ☒

$$x _ { k + 1 } = x _ { k } + d u \left( x _ { k } \right) .$$ ☒
(A.5)

The second method is the Newton-Raphson method which, like the
method of steepest ascent, starts with an initial guess at the point which
maximizes $f \left( x \right) .$ After $k$ steps of the algorithm, the updated estimate
☒ ☒
of the point which maximizes $f \left( \mathrm { x } \right)$ is given by
☒ ☒

$$\mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } = \mathrm { x } _ { k } - \mathrm { H } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { u } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) .$$ ☒ ☒
(A.6)

The Newton-Raphson algorithm converges quite rapidly when the ini-
tial guess is not too far from the maximum. When the initial guess is
poor, the algorithm may move in the wrong direction or may take a step
in the correct direction, but overshoot the root. The value of the function
should be computed at each step to ensure that the algorithm is moving
in the correct direction. If $f \left( x _ { k } \right)$ is smaller than $f \left( x _ { k + 1 } \right) ,$ one option is
☒ ☒

to cut the step size in half and try $\mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } = \mathrm { x } _ { k } - \mathrm { H } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { u } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) \bar { / } 2 .$ This
procedure is used in SAS and BMDP in the Cox regression procedure.
☒ ☒

The third method is Marquardt's (1963) compromise between the
method of steepest ascent and the Newton-Raphson method. This

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageHeader="A.2 Multivariate Methods 447" -->

method uses a constant, $\gamma ,$ which blends the two methods together.
When $\gamma$ is zero, the method reduces to the Newton-Raphson method,
and, as $\gamma \rightarrow \infty ,$ the method approaches the method of steepest as-
cent. Again, the method starts with an initial guess, $\mathrm { X } _ { o } .$ Let $\mathrm { S } _ { k }$ be the
$p \times p$ diagonal scaling matrix with diagonal element $\left( | \mathrm { H } _ { i i } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) | ^ { - 1 / 2 } \right) .$ The
updated estimate of the maximum is given by

$$\mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } = \mathrm { x } _ { k } - \mathrm { S } _ { k } \left( \mathrm { S } _ { k } \mathrm { H } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) \mathrm { S } _ { k } + \gamma \mathrm { I } \right) ^ { - 1 } \mathrm { S } _ { k } \mathrm { u } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) ,$$

where $I$ is the identity matrix. Typically, the algorithm is implemented
with a small value of $\gamma$ for the first iteration. If $f \left( x _ { 1 } \right) < f \left( x _ { 0 } \right) ,$ then,
we are having difficulty approaching the maximum and the value of
$\gamma$ is increased until $f \left( x _ { 1 } \right) > f \left( x _ { 0 } \right) .$ This procedure is iterated until
convergence is attained. For the final step of the algorithm, a "Newton-
Raphson" step with $\gamma = 0$ is taken to ensure convergence.

In the multivariate maximization problem, there are several sugges-
tions for declaring convergence of these algorithms. These include stop-
ping $f \left( x _ { k + 1 } \right) - f \left( x _ { k } \right) < \epsilon$ $\left. | \left[ f \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } \right) - f \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) \right] / f \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) | < \epsilon \right)$
☒
when $\begin{array}{} \mathrm { v n e n } J \left( \mathrm { X } _ { k + 1 } \right) - J \left( \mathrm { X } _ { k } \right) < \epsilon \left( \mathrm { O r } | \mathrm { l } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } \right) - J \left( \mathrm { X } _ { k } \right) | f _ { \mathrm { I } } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k } \right) | < \epsilon \right) \\ \sum _ { \mathrm { I } } \mathrm { u } _ { j } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } < \epsilon \left( \mathrm { o r } \max \left[ | \mathrm { u } _ { \mathrm { l } } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } \right) | , \ldots , | \mathrm { u } _ { \mathrm { p } } \left( \mathrm { x } _ { k + 1 } \right) | \right] < \epsilon \right) \mathrm { o } \end{array}$
when $\sum \left( x _ { k + 1 , j } - x _ { k , j } \right) ^ { 2 } < \epsilon$ (or max|Xk+1,1 - Xk,1 , ., |Xk+1,p - Xk.p| < E).

We shall fit a two-parameter Weibull model with survival function
$S \left( t \right) = \exp \left( - \lambda t ^ { \alpha } \right)$ to the ten observations in Example A.2. Here the
log likelihood function is given by

$$L \left( \lambda , \alpha \right) = n \ln \lambda + n \ln \alpha + \left( \alpha - 1 \right) \sum \ln t _ { i } - \lambda \sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha } .$$

The score vector $\mathrm { u } \left( \lambda , \alpha \right)$ is expressed by

$$u _ { \lambda } \left( \lambda , \alpha \right) = \frac { \partial L \left( \lambda , \alpha \right) } { \partial \lambda } = \frac { n } { \lambda } - \sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha }$$
$$u _ { \alpha } \left( \lambda , \alpha \right) = \frac { \partial L \left( \lambda , \alpha \right) } { \partial \alpha } = \frac { n } { \alpha } + \sum \ln t _ { i } - \lambda \sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \ln t _ { i }$$

and the Hessian matrix is

$$H \left( \lambda , \alpha \right) = \left( \begin{array}{} { - \frac { n } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } } & { - \sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \ln t _ { i } } \\ { - \sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \ln t _ { i } } & { - \frac { n } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } - \lambda \sum t _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \left( \ln t _ { i } ^ { 2 } \right) } \end{array} \right)$$

To apply the method of steepest ascent, we must find the value of $d _ { k }$
which maximizes $L \left[ \left( \lambda _ { k } + d _ { k } u _ { \lambda } \left[ \lambda _ { k } , \alpha _ { k } \right] \right) \right. ,$ $\left. \left( \alpha _ { k } + d _ { k } u _ { \alpha } \left[ \lambda _ { k } , \alpha _ { k } \right] \right) \right] .$ This needs
to be done numerically and this example uses a Newton-Raphson $a l -$
gorithm. Convergence of the algorithm is declared when the maximum
of $| u _ { \lambda } |$ $| u _ { \alpha } |$ is less than 0.1. Starting with an initial guess of $\alpha = 1$
and $\lambda = 1 0 / \sum t _ { i } = 1 . 0 2 4 ,$ which leads to a log likelihood $o f \quad - 9 . 7 5 7 ,$
we have the following results:

EXAMPLE A.2
