<!-- PageBreak -->


## B Large-Sample Tests Based on Likelihood Theory

Many of the test procedures used in survival analysis are based on
the asymptotic properties of the likelihood or the partial likelihood.
These test procedures are based on either the maximized likelihood
itself (likelihood ratio tests), on the estimators standardized by use of
the information matrix (Wald tests), or on the first derivatives of the
log likelihood (score tests). In this appendix, we will review how these
tests are constructed. See Chapter 9 of Cox and Hinkley (1974) for a
more detailed reference.

Let $\mathrm { Y }$ denote the data and $\theta = \left( \theta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \theta _ { D } \right)$ be the parameter vector.
Let $L \left( \theta : \mathrm { Y } \right)$ denote either the likelihood or partial likelihood function.
The maximum likelihood estimator of $\theta$ is the function of the data
which maximizes the likelihood, that is, $\widehat { \theta } \left( \mathrm { Y } \right) = \widehat { \theta }$ is the value of $\theta$
which maximizes $L \left( \theta : \mathrm { Y } \right)$ or, equivalently, maximizes $\log L \left( \theta : \mathrm { Y } \right) .$

Associated with the likelihood function is the efficient score vector
$\mathrm { U } \left( \theta \right) = \left[ U _ { 1 } \left( \theta \right) , \ldots , U _ { p } \left( \theta \right) \right]$ defined by

$$U _ { j } \left( \theta \right) = \frac { \delta } { \delta \theta _ { j } } \ln L \left( \theta : Y \right) .$$
(B.1)

In most regular cases, the maximum likelihood estimator is the solution
to the equation $\mathrm { U } \left( \theta \right) = 0 .$ The efficient score vector has the property

<!-- PageNumber="449" -->
<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="450" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix B Large-Sample Tests Based on Likelihood Theory" -->

that its expected value is zero when the expectation is taken with
respect to the true value of 0.

A second key quantity in large-sample likelihood theory is the Fisher
information matrix defined by

$$\mathrm { i } \left( \theta \right) = E _ { \theta } \left[ \mathrm { U } \left( \theta \right) ^ { i } \mathrm { U } \left( \theta \right) \right] = E _ { \theta } \left[ - \frac { \delta } { \delta \theta } \mathrm { U } \left( \theta \right) \right]$$
$$= \left\{ - E _ { \theta } \left[ \frac { \delta ^ { 2 } } { \delta \theta _ { j } \delta \theta _ { k } } \ln L \left( \theta : \mathrm { Y } \right) \right] \right\} , j = 1 , \ldots , p , k = 1 , \ldots , p . \left( \mathrm { B } . \right. ,$$

Computation of the expectation in (B.2) is very difficult in most appli-
cations of likelihood theory, so a consistent estimator of i is used. This
estimator is the observed information, $\mathbb{I} \left( \theta \right) ,$ whose $\left( j , \right.$ k)th element is
given by

$$I _ { j , k } \left( \theta \right) = - \frac { \delta ^ { 2 } \ln L \left( \theta : \mathrm { Y } \right) } { \delta \theta _ { j } \delta \theta _ { k } } , j , k = 1 , \ldots , p .$$
(B.3)

The first set of tests based on the likelihood are for the simple null
hypothesis, $H _ { o } : \theta = \theta _ { o } .$ The first test is the likelihood ratio test based
on the statistic

$$\chi _ { L R } ^ { 2 } = - 2 \left[ \ln L \left( \theta _ { o } : Y \right) - \ln L \left( \widehat { \theta } : Y \right) \right]$$
(B.4)

This statistic has an asymptotic chi-squared distribution with $p$ degrees
of freedom under the null hypothesis.

A second test, called the Wald test, is based on the large-sample
distribution of the maximum likelihood estimator. For large samples,
$\widehat { \theta }$ has a multivariate normal distribution with mean $\theta$ and covariance
matrix $\mathrm { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta \right)$ so the quadratic form $\left( \widehat { \theta } - \theta _ { o } \right) \mathrm { i } \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) \left( \widehat { \theta } - \theta _ { o } \right) ^ { t }$ has a chi-
squared distribution with $p$ degrees of freedom for large samples. Using
the observed information as an estimator of the Fisher information, the
Wald statistic is expressed as

$$\chi _ { W } ^ { 2 } = \left( \widehat { \theta } - \theta _ { o } \right) \mathbb{I} \left( \widehat { \theta } \right) \left( \widehat { \theta } - \theta _ { o } \right) ^ { t }$$
(B.5)

which has a chi-squared distribution with p degrees of freedom for
large samples when $H _ { c }$ is true.

The third test, called the score or Rao test, is based on the efficient
score statistics. When $\theta = \theta _ { o } ,$ the score vector $\mathrm { U } \left( \theta _ { o } \right)$ has a large-sample
multivariate normal distribution with mean 0 and covariance matrix
$\mathrm { i } \left( \theta \right) .$ This leads to a test statistic given by

$$\chi _ { S } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { U } \left( \theta _ { o } \right) \mathrm { i } ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta _ { o } \right) \mathrm { U } ^ { t } \left( \theta _ { o } \right) .$$

As for the Wald test, the Fisher information is replaced in most applica-
tions by the observed information, so the test statistic is given by

$$\chi _ { S } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { U } \left( \theta _ { o } \right) \mathrm { I } ^ { - 1 } \left( \theta _ { o } \right) \mathrm { U } ^ { t } \left( \theta _ { o } \right) .$$
(B.6)

Again, this statistic has an asymptotic chi-squared distribution with $p$
degrees of freedom when $H _ { o }$ is true. The score test has an advantage in

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="451" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix B Large-Sample Tests Based on Likelihood Theory" -->

many applications in that the maximum likelihood estimates need not
be calculated.


### EXAMPLE B.1

Suppose we have a censored sample of size $n$ from an exponential
population with hazard rate $\lambda .$ We wish to test the $\mathrm { h y p o t h e s i s }$ that
$\lambda = 1 .$ Let $\left( T _ { i } , \delta _ { i } \right) ,$ $i = 1 , \ldots , n ,$ so that the likelihood, $L \left( \lambda ; \left( T _ { i } , \delta _ { i } \right) \right. ,$ $i =$
$1 , \ldots , n ,$ is given by $\prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda ^ { \delta _ { i } } e ^ { - \lambda T _ { i } } = \lambda ^ { D } e ^ { - \lambda S }$ where $D = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \delta _ { i } ,$ is the
observed number of deaths and $S = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } T _ { i }$ is the total time on test
(see Section 3.5). Thus,

$$\ln L \left( \lambda \right) = D \ln \lambda - \lambda S ,$$
(B.7)

$$U \left( \lambda \right) = \frac { d } { d \lambda } \ln L \left( \lambda \right) = \frac { D } { \lambda } - S ,$$
(B.8)

and

$$I \left( \lambda \right) = - \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d \lambda ^ { 2 } } \ln L \left( \lambda \right) = \frac { D } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } .$$
(B.9)

Solving B.8 for $\lambda$ gives us the maximum likelihood estimator, $\widehat { \lambda } = D / S .$
Using these statistics,

$$\chi _ { S } ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { D } { 1 } - S \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \left( \frac { 1 ^ { 2 } } { D } \right) = \frac { \left( D - S \right) ^ { 2 } } { D } ,$$
$$\chi _ { W } ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { D } { S } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \frac { D } { \left( D / S \right) ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( D - S \right) ^ { 2 } } { D }$$
$$\chi _ { L R } ^ { 2 } = - 2 \left\{ \left( D \ln 1 - 1 \cdot S \right) - \left[ D \ln \left( D / S \right) - \left( D / S \right) \cdot S \right] \right\}$$
$$= 2 \left[ S - D + D \ln \left( D / S \right) \right]$$

In this case, note that the Wald and Rao tests are identical. All three
of these statistics have asymptotic chi-squared distributions with one
degree of freedom.

All three test statistics can be used to test composite hypotheses.
Suppose the parameter vector 0 is divided into two vectors $\psi$ and $\phi$
of lengths $p _ { 1 } ,$ and $p _ { 2 } ,$ respectively. We would like to test the hypoth-
esis $H _ { 0 } : \psi = \psi _ { o } .$ Here $\phi$ is a nuisance parameter. Let $\widehat { \phi } \left( \psi _ { o } \right)$ be the
maximum likelihood estimates of $\phi$ obtained by maximizing the likeli-
$h o o d$ with respect to $\phi ,$ with $\psi$ fixed at $\psi _ { o }$ .. That is, $\widehat { \phi } \left( \psi _ { o } \right)$ maximizes
$\ln L \left[ \left( \psi _ { o } , \phi \right) : \mathrm { Y } \right]$ with respect to $\phi .$ We also partition the information
matrix I into

$$\mathrm { I } = \left( \begin{array}{} \mathrm { I } _ { \psi \psi } & \mathrm { I } _ { \psi \phi } \\ \mathrm { I } _ { \phi \psi } & \mathrm { I } _ { \phi \phi } \end{array} \right) ,$$
(B.10)

where $I _ { v u l }$ is of dimension $p _ { 1 } \times p _ { 1 } ,$ $\mathrm { I } _ { \Phi \Phi }$ is of dimension $p _ { 2 } \times p _ { 2 } ,$ $I _ { v d }$
is $p _ { 1 } \times p _ { 2 } ,$ and $\mathrm { I } _ { \phi \psi } ^ { t } = \mathrm { I } _ { \psi \phi } .$ Notice that a partitioned information matrix

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="452" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix B Large-Sample Tests Based on Likelihood Theory" -->

has an inverse which is also a partitioned matrix with

$$I ^ { - 1 } = \left( \begin{array}{} { I ^ { \psi } V } & { I ^ { \psi \phi } } \\ { I ^ { \phi \psi } } & { I ^ { \phi \phi } } \end{array} \right)$$
(B.11)

With these refinements, the three statistics for testing $H _ { 0 } : \psi = \psi _ { c }$ are
given by

Likelihood ratio test:

$$\chi _ { L R } ^ { 2 } = - 2 \left\{ \ln L \left[ \left( \psi _ { o } , \widehat { \phi } \left( \psi _ { o } \right) : \mathrm { Y } \right) \right] - \ln L \left( \widehat { \theta } : \mathrm { Y } \right) \right\} ,$$
(B.12)

Wald test:

$$\chi _ { W } ^ { 2 } = \left( \widehat { \psi } - \psi _ { o } \right) \left[ \mathrm { I } ^ { \psi \psi } \left( \widehat { \psi } , \widehat { \phi } \right) \right] ^ { - 1 } \left( \widehat { \psi } - \psi _ { o } \right) ^ { t } ,$$
(B.13)

and score test:

$$\chi _ { S } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { U } _ { \psi } \left[ \psi _ { o } , \widehat { \phi } \left( \psi _ { o } \right) \right] \left[ \mathrm { I } ^ { \psi \psi } \left( \psi _ { o } , \widehat { \phi } \left( \psi _ { o } \right) \right) \right] \mathrm { U } ^ { t } \left[ \psi _ { o } , \widehat { \phi } \left( \psi _ { o } \right) \right] .$$
(B.14)

All three statistics have an asymptotic chi-squared distribution with $p _ { 1 }$
degrees of freedom when the null hypothesis is true.


### EXAMPLE B.2

Consider the problem of comparing two treatments, where the time to
event in each group has an exponential distribution. For population
one, we assume that the hazard rate is $\lambda$ whereas for population two,
we assume that the hazard rate is $\lambda \beta .$ We shall test $H _ { o } : \beta = 1$ treating
$\lambda$ as a nuisance parameter. The likelihood function is given by

$$L \left[ \left( \lambda , \beta \right) : D _ { 1 } , D _ { 2 } , S _ { 1 } , S _ { 2 } \right] = \lambda ^ { D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } } \beta ^ { D _ { 2 } } \exp \left( - \lambda S _ { 1 } - \lambda \beta S _ { 2 } \right)$$
(B.15)

where $D i$ is the number of events and $S i$ is the total time on test in the
ith sample, $i = 1 ,$ 2. From (B.15),

$$\ln L \left[ \left( \beta , \lambda \right) \right] = \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) \ln \lambda + D _ { 2 } \ln \beta - \lambda S _ { 1 } - \lambda \beta S _ { 2 } ,$$
(B.16)

$$U _ { \beta } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = \frac { \delta } { \delta \beta } \ln L \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = \frac { D _ { 2 } } { \beta } - \lambda S _ { 2 } ,$$
$$U _ { \lambda } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = \frac { \delta } { \delta \lambda } \ln L \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = \frac { D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } } { \lambda } - S _ { 1 } - \beta S _ { 2 } ,$$
(B.17)
(B.18)

$$I _ { \beta \beta } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = - \frac { \delta ^ { 2 } \ln L \left( \beta , \lambda \right) } { \delta \beta ^ { 2 } } = \frac { D _ { 2 } } { \beta ^ { 2 } } ,$$
(B.19)

$$I _ { \lambda \lambda } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = - \frac { \delta ^ { 2 } \ln L \left( \beta , \lambda \right) } { \delta \lambda ^ { 2 } } = \frac { D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } } ,$$
(B.20)

and

$$I _ { \beta \lambda } = - \frac { \delta ^ { 2 } \ln L \left( \beta , \lambda \right) } { \delta \lambda \delta \beta } = S _ { 2 } .$$
(B.21)

equations $U _ { \beta } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = 0 ,$ $U _ { \lambda } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = 0$ yields the
$$\widetilde { \mathrm { g l o b a l } } \mathrm { m a x i m u m l i k e l i b o d } \mathrm { e s t i m a t o r s } \widehat { \beta } = S _ { 1 } D _ { 2 } / \left( S _ { 2 } D _ { 1 } \right) \mathrm { a n d } \widehat { \lambda } = D _ { 1 } / S _ { 1 } .$$

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="453" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix B Large-Sample Tests Based on Likelihood Theory" -->

Solving $U _ { \lambda } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = 0 ,$ for $\beta$ fixed at its value under $H _ { 0 } ,$ yields $\widehat { \lambda } \left( \beta = 1 \right) ,$
denoted by $\widehat { \lambda } \left( 1 \right) , = \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) / \left( S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } \right) .$ Thus, we have from B.12 a
$l i k e l i h o o d$ ratio test statistic of

$$\chi _ { I R } ^ { 2 } = - 2 \left\{ \left[ \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) \ln \left[ \widehat { \lambda } \left( 1 \right) \right] - \widehat { \lambda } \left( 1 \right) \left( S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } \right) \right] \right.$$
$$\left. - \left[ \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) \ln \left[ \widehat { \lambda } \right] + D _ { 2 } \ln \left[ \widehat { \beta } \right] - \widehat { \lambda } S _ { 1 } - \widehat { \lambda } \widehat { \beta } S _ { 2 } \right] \right\} .$$
$$= 2 D _ { 1 } \ln \left[ \frac { D _ { 1 } \left( S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } \right) } { S _ { 1 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) } \right] + 2 D _ { 2 } \ln \left[ \frac { D _ { 2 } \left( S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } \right) } { S _ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) } \right] .$$

From B.19-B.21,

$$I ^ { \beta \beta } \left( \beta , \lambda \right) = \frac { \beta ^ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) } { \left[ D _ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) - \left( \lambda \beta S _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] }$$

so, the Wald test is given by

$$\chi _ { W } ^ { 2 } = \left( \widehat { \beta } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } \left\{ \frac { \widehat { \beta } ^ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) } { \left[ D _ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) - \left( \widehat { \lambda } \widehat { \beta } S _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] } \right\} ^ { - 1 }$$
$$= \frac { D _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( S _ { 1 } D _ { 2 } - S _ { 2 } D _ { 1 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { D _ { 2 } S _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) }$$

The score test is given by

$$\chi _ { S } ^ { 2 } = \left( D _ { 2 } - \widehat { \lambda } \left( 1 \right) S _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) } { \left[ D _ { 2 } \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) - \left( \widehat { \lambda } \left( 1 \right) S _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] }$$
$$= \frac { \left[ D _ { 2 } \left( S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } \right) - \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) S _ { 2 } \right] ^ { 2 } } { D _ { 2 } \left( S _ { 1 } + S _ { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( D _ { 1 } + D _ { 2 } \right) S _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }$$

If, for example, $D _ { 1 } = 1 0 ,$ $D _ { 2 } = 1 2 ,$ $S _ { 1 } = 2 5 ,$ and $S _ { 2 } = 2 7 ,$ then
$\chi _ { L R } ^ { 2 } = 0 . 0 6 0 7 ,$ $\chi _ { W } ^ { 2 } = 0 . 0 5 4 5$ and $\chi _ { S } ^ { 2 } = 0 . 0 4 4 8 ,$ all nonsignificant when
compared to a chi-square with one degree of freedom.

<table>
<caption>TABLE C.3b Confidence Coefficients $c _ { 0 5 } \left( a _ { L } , a _ { U } \right)$ for 95% EP Confidence Bands</caption>
<tr>
<th rowspan="2">$a _ { U }$</th>
<th colspan="2" rowspan="2">0.02 0.04</th>
<th colspan="8">$a _ { 1 }$</th>

<!-- PageNumber="484" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix D Data on 137 Bone Marrow Transplant Patients" -->

TABLE D.1
Data on 137 Bone Marrow Transplant Patients

g-Disease group

1-ALL

2-AML low-risk

3-AML high-risk

T1-Time (in days) to death or on study time

T2-Disease-Free survival time (time to relapse, death or end of study)

$\begin{array}{} { } ^ { 8 } 1 - \text { Death indicator } \\ 1 - \text { Dead } \quad 0 - \text { Aliv } 6 \end{array}$

82-Relapse indicator

1-Relapsed
0-Disease-Free

83-Disease-Free survival indicator
1-Dead or relapsed
0-Alive disease-free

TA-Time (in days) to acute graft-versus-host disease

8A-Acute graft-versus-host disease indicator

1-Developed acute graft-versus-host disease
0-Never developed acute graft-versus-host disease

Tc-Time (in days) to chronic graft-versus-host disease

8c-Chronic graft-versus-host disease indicator

1-Developed chronic graft-versus-host disease

0-Never developed chronic graft-versus-host disease

Tp-Time (in days) to return of platelets to normal levels

8p-Platelet recovery indicator

1-Platelets returned to normal levels

0-Platelets never returned to normal levels

Z1-Patient age in years

Z2-Donor age in years

Z3-Patient sex

1-Male
0-Female

Z4-Donor Sex

1-Male
0-Female

Z5-Patient CMV status

1-CMV positive
0-CMV negative

Z6-Donor CMV status

1-CMV positive
0-CMV negative

$Z$ -- Waiting time to transplant in days

Z8-FAB

1-FAB Grade 4 Or 5 and AML
0-Otherwise

Z9-Hospital

1-The Ohio State University
2-Alfred

3-St. Vincent
4-Hahnemann

Z10-MTX used as a graft-versus-host-prophylactic
1-Yes
0-No

$$\left[ 1 - \frac { 1 } { 1 + \lambda \left( 0 . 2 5 \right) ^ { \alpha } } \right] \left[ 1 - \frac { 1 } { 1 + \lambda \left( 1 . 2 5 \right) ^ { \alpha } } \right]$$

(c) The Kaplan-Meier estimator (KME) for thymic lymphoma estimates
the probability of having died from this cause in a hypothetical
world where no other cause of death is possible.

(d) At 500 days we have 0.0759, at 800 days we have 0.6000. These
are estimates of the conditional probability of dying from thymic
lymphoma among survivors who have not died from one of the
other two causes of death.

Likelihood ratio p-value $= 0 . 0 0 7$ and Wald p-value $= 0 . 0 0 6 .$ Thus
we conclude that there is a significant interaction between disease
type and transplant type.

(c) Either model gives a relative risk for an NHL Auto transplant patient
to an NHL Allo transplant patient of 1.94 with a 95% confidence
interval of (0.64, 5.87).

(d) Comparing Allo patients gives a p-value of 0.007. Comparing Auto
patients gives a p-value of 0.31.

(e) The test statistic is 8.50, which has a chi-square distribution with 2
degrees of freedom and the p-value is 0.014.

Note that the inferential conclusions in this entire problem do not de-
pend upon the coding scheme employed.

$p - v a l u e = 0 . 0 5 6 .$

(b) $p \mathrm { - v a l u e } = 0 . 0 7 7 .$

(d) The final model along with the parameter estimates is given below.
Although we have used Wald tests in this exercise, similar conclu-
sions are obtained if the likelihood ratio statistic is used throughout.

The local Wald test of the primary hypothesis of no difference
between the times to staphylococcus infection for the two disinfec-
tant groups has a p-value of 0.044, which suggests that the times
to staphylococcus infection are different for the two disinfectant
groups after adjustment for the type of the burn and race of the
patient. Note that the test was insignificant when no cofounders
were adjusted for in part a.

8.13
At 20 days for a patient with 25% of the total body area burned, 95%
confidence intervals for the survival functions based on the log trans-
formation (4.3.2), for the two bathing solutions, are $\left( 0 . 5 2 , 0 . 7 5 \right)$ and
(0.67,0.85), respectively.


### Solutions to Chapter 9

9.1
Using $g \left( t \right) = \log \left( t \right)$ the Wald p-value is 0.47. No evidence of a departure
from proportional hazards.

9.3
(a) Relative $\mathrm { R i s k } = 0 . 9 0 ,$ Confidence $I n t e r v a l = \left( 0 . 5 8 , 1 . 3 9 \right)$

(b) Using $g \left( t \right) = \log \left( t \right)$ the Wald p-value is 0.009.

(c) The best cut point is at 254 days.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="502" -->

(d) Up to 254 days the relative risk of Chemo only to Chemo + Radiation
is 0.24 95% Confidence Interval (0.10, 0.56). Among 254-day sur-
vivors the relative risk is 1.89 $\left( 1 0 4 , 3 . 4 4 \right) .$

11.5
(a) The deviance residual suggests that there are a number of data
points for which the model does not fit well. These are points
with a deviance residual greater than 2. The worst three points are
observations 529, 527, 526, which all have a risk score $o f \quad - 0 . 2 4 8 .$
These patients, who the model predicts are good-risk patients, die
very early at 2, 3, and 7 days after transplant. Most of the "outliers"
are of this nature, patients who died too soon after transplant. A
possible remedy is to add additional covariates which could help
to explain these early deaths.

(b) For race these are observations 444, 435, 434, and 433. All are the
four black males with the shortest survival time. For gender these
are observations 532, 529, 527, and 526, the four white females who
die the soonest after transplant. For the interaction term these are
observations 812, 809, 807, and 806, the four black females with
the shortest survival time.

### Solutions to Chapter 12

12.1
(a) For aneuploid group, $\widehat { \lambda } = 0 . 0 1 6 ,$ $\widehat { \alpha } = 0 . 8 3 2$ with standard errors
0.010 and 0.128, respectively, and for diploid group, $\widehat { \lambda } = 0 . 0 3 6 ,$
$\widehat { \alpha } = 0 . 7 7 5$ with standard errors 0.023 and 0.136, respectively.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="509" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix E Selected Solutions to Exercises" -->

(b) For aneuploid group, L.R. p-value is 0.21 and Wald p-value is 0.27;
for diploid group, L.R. p-value is 0.12 and Wald p-value is 0.19.

(c) MLE of median of aneuploid and diploid groups, respectively, are
91.8 and 45.8. SE of median of aneuploid and diploid groups, re-
spectively, are 19.8 and 17.6.

(d) LR and Wald p-values are 0.059 and 0.057, respectively. Estimate of
RR is 0.58 and the 95% confidence interval is $\left( 0 . 3 4 , 1 . 0 1 \right) .$ Estimate
of the acceleration factor is 0.51 and the 95% confidence interval is
(0.26, 1.02). This means that the median lifetime for diploid patients
is between 0.26 and 1.02 times that of aneuploid patients with 95%
confidence.

</tr>

(b) $p - v a l u e < 0 . 0 0 1 .$

(c) $R R = 3 . 4$ and 95% confidence interval for $R R$ is (1.1, 10.6).

(d) $p \mathrm { - v a l u e } < 0 . 0 0 1$ and 0.17, respectively.

(e) Using contrast matrix $C = \left( \begin{array}{} { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right) ,$ $p - v a l u e < 0 . 0 0 1 .$

12.5
(a) For aneuploid group, $\widehat { \lambda } = 0 . 0 0 9 ,$ $\widehat { \alpha } = 1 . 0 4 8$ with standard errors
0.007 and 0.163, respectively, and for diploid group, $\widehat { \lambda } = 0 . 0 2 2 ,$
$\widehat { \alpha } = 1 . 0 3 5$ with standard errors 0.016 and 0.181, respectively.

(b) $p - v a l u e > 0 . 6$ for both aneuploid and diploid groups.

<!-- PageBreak -->

<!-- PageNumber="510" -->
<!-- PageHeader="Appendix E Selected Solutions to Exercises" -->

(c) MLE of median of aneuploid and diploid groups, respectively, are
87.2 and 39.6. SE of median of aneuploid and diploid groups, re-
spectively, are 21.2 and 12.9.

(d) LR and Wald p-values are both 0.051. Estimate of relative $\mathrm { o d d s }$ is
0.44 and the 95% confidence interval is (0.19,1.01). Estimate of
the acceleration factor is 0.45 and the 95% confidence interval is
(0.21, 1.002).

12.7
(a) For aneuploid group $\widehat { \mu } = 4 . 4 6 ,$ $\widehat { \sigma } = 1 . 7 2 ,$ and var-cov matrix for $\widehat { \mu }$
and $\widehat { \sigma }$ is

0.07 0.02
0.02 0.06

For diploid group $\widehat { \mu } = 3 . 6 4 ,$ $\widehat { \sigma } = 1 . 6 3 4$ and var-cov matrix for $\widehat { \mu }$
and $\widehat { \sigma }$ is

0.10 0.01
0.01 0.07

(b) For aneuploid group $\widehat { \mu } = 1 1 2 . 2 1 ,$ $\widehat { \sigma } = 9 6 . 7 1$ and var-cov matrix for
$\widehat { \mu }$ and $\widehat { \sigma }$ is

$$\begin{array}{} { 2 3 3 . 2 2 } & { 6 1 . 4 7 } \\ { 6 1 . 4 7 } & { 1 6 9 . 4 4 } \end{array}$$

For diploid group $\widehat { \mu } = 7 1 . 3 2 ,$ $\widehat { \sigma } = 7 0 . 6 7$ and var-cov matrix for $\widehat { \mu }$
and $\widehat { \sigma }$ is

$$\begin{array}{} { 1 9 4 . 2 7 } & { 1 9 . 8 9 } \\ { 1 9 . 8 9 } & { 1 2 1 . 0 3 } \end{array}$$

(c) For aneuploid group $\widehat { \mu } = 4 . 7 5 ,$ $\widehat { \sigma } = 1 . 4 4 ,$ $\widehat { \theta } = 0 . 5 3$ and var-cov

is 0.39 and 0.69, respectively. Cannot reject $\log$ normal fit for both

<!-- PageNumber="511" -->

(e) For aneuploid and diploid group the Wald p-values for testing $\widehat { \theta } = 1$
are 0.75 and 0.70, $\widetilde { \mathrm { r e s p e c t i v e l y } } .$ Cannot reject Weibull fit for both
groups.

12.9
The hazard plots, all of which should be linear, suggest that any of the
models would be reasonable.

12.13

logistic model. Both Deviance residual plots suggest the models do not
fit well to early events.

### Solutions to Chapter 13

13.1
$T = 1 4 . 8 ,$ $V = 1 0 7 . 5 ,$ $Z = 1 . 4 ,$ $p = 0 . 1 5 3 9 .$ No evidence of random
effect.

13.3
(a) Standard Cox model $b = - \quad 1 . 0 3 5 ,$ SE $\left( b \right) = 0 . 4 4 ,$ $p = 0 . 0 1 8 7 .$

(b) Gamma Frailty Model $b = - \quad 1 . 3 0 5 ,$ SE $\left( b \right) = 0 . 5 2 8 ,$ $p = 0 . 0 1 3 3 .$
Estimate of $\theta = 0 . 7 1 3 ,$ $\mathrm { S E } = 0 . 6 2 2 ,$ $\mathrm { w a l d } t$ p-value of test of $\theta = 0 =$
0.2517, likelihood ratio $p - v a l u e = 0 . 1 2 8 6 .$

13.5
(a) See 13.1.

(b) Adjusted $\mathrm { S E } = 0 . 3 8 5 2 ,$ test $\mathrm { s t a t i s t i c } = - 1 . 0 3 5 / 0 . 3 8 5 2 ,$ $p = . 0 0 7 2 .$
